front: What is the difference between a mutex, a semaphore, and a read-write lock? When do you use each?
back: Mutex (mutual exclusion lock): binary lock with ownership — only the thread that acquired it can release it. Enforces exclusive access to a critical section. Use for protecting shared mutable state where exactly one writer/reader is safe at a time. Semaphore: a counter with P (wait/decrement) and V (signal/increment) operations, no ownership concept. Used for signaling between threads (one signals, another waits) or limiting concurrent access to N resources (e.g., connection pool of size 10). A binary semaphore can act like a mutex but lacks ownership semantics — any thread can signal it, which is a common bug source. Read-write lock (RWLock): allows N concurrent readers OR 1 exclusive writer. Use when reads dominate and writes are rare (e.g., config lookup, in-memory cache). Failure mode: writer starvation if readers arrive continuously — good implementations prioritize pending writers.
senior_nuance: Mutex ownership enables priority inheritance — if a high-priority thread waits on a mutex held by a low-priority thread, the kernel temporarily boosts the holder's priority to prevent priority inversion (the classic Mars Pathfinder bug). Semaphores cannot do this. In Linux futex-based mutexes (pthreads), priority inheritance requires PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK or PTHREAD_MUTEX_ROBUST attributes explicitly.
quote_to_say: A mutex has an owner; a semaphore has a count — that single difference has caused countless bugs.

front: State the four Coffman conditions for deadlock. Which one is easiest to break in practice?
back: All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock: (1) Mutual Exclusion — resources are held exclusively (only one thread at a time). (2) Hold and Wait — a thread holds at least one resource while waiting for additional resources. (3) No Preemption — resources cannot be forcibly taken; they must be released voluntarily. (4) Circular Wait — a cycle exists in the resource-dependency graph (T1 waits for R1 held by T2, T2 waits for R2 held by T1). Breaking any one prevents deadlock. Easiest to break: Circular Wait — establish a global lock ordering and always acquire locks in that order. If every thread acquires mutex A before mutex B, the cycle cannot form. Also commonly broken: Hold and Wait — acquire all needed locks atomically (try-lock pattern) or release all before requesting more.
senior_nuance: Lock ordering is the most practical prevention but requires discipline across a codebase. Tools like ThreadSanitizer (TSan) detect lock-order inversions dynamically. Databases use deadlock detection (cycle detection in the waits-for graph) and abort one of the transactions as the victim — more flexible than static prevention. Timeout-based deadlock recovery (try_lock_for) trades reliability for latency predictability.
quote_to_say: Break circular wait with a global lock order — simple rule, consistent enforcement.

front: What is a data race, and how does it differ from a race condition?
back: A data race (the formal definition): two threads access the same memory location concurrently, at least one access is a write, and there is no synchronization between them. Data races are undefined behavior in C/C++ and Java — the compiler and hardware may reorder, cache, or elide operations, producing unpredictable results. A race condition is a broader logical bug: the program outcome depends on the relative ordering of events, even if no data race exists. Example: a check-then-act pattern (if file doesn't exist, create it) can race without any data race if two threads both see 'no file' before either creates it. All data races are bugs; not all race conditions involve data races. ThreadSanitizer (TSan) detects data races. Race conditions require higher-level reasoning (invariant checking, idempotency design).
senior_nuance: The double-checked locking pattern for singletons is a classic example of a race-condition-free program with a data race: the second check happens without a lock, allowing a CPU to observe a partially constructed object through a stale cache line. In C++11+, this is fixed with std::atomic<T*> with acquire/release semantics. In Java, use volatile. In Go, all shared state must be accessed under a mutex or through channels — the race detector (-race) catches violations.
quote_to_say: Data races are undefined behavior. Race conditions are logic bugs. Know which you have.

front: Explain acquire/release/seq-cst memory ordering. When does each apply?
back: Memory ordering controls how compiler and CPU reorder reads/writes relative to synchronization points. Relaxed: no ordering guarantees beyond atomicity — use only for statistics counters where order is irrelevant. Acquire (load): no read/write in the current thread may be reordered before this load — anything 'below' the load is visible after it. Use for lock acquire. Release (store): no read/write in the current thread may be reordered after this store — anything 'above' the store is published before it. Use for lock release. Acquire + Release together form a synchronization pair: a release store on thread A followed by an acquire load of the same value on thread B creates a happens-before relationship — B sees all writes A did before its release. Sequentially consistent (seq-cst): all threads agree on a single total order of all seq-cst operations, as if there were one global lock. Strongest and most expensive — requires full memory barriers on x86/ARM. Use only when you need global ordering across multiple atomic variables.
senior_nuance: On x86, loads are always acquire and stores are always release (TSO — total store order) — relaxed and acquire/release atomics compile to the same instructions. ARM and POWER are weakly ordered — acquire/release compiles to load-acquire (LDAR) and store-release (STLR) instructions. Seq-cst requires MFENCE on x86 or DMB ISH on ARM — measurably slower. Most lock-free data structures only need acquire/release; seq-cst is rarely necessary in practice.
quote_to_say: Acquire says: see everything before the release that published this value.

front: What is the happens-before relation, and why does it matter for reasoning about concurrent programs?
back: Happens-before (HB) is a formal partial order over events in a concurrent execution. If A happens-before B, then B is guaranteed to observe all effects of A. Rules that establish HB: (1) within a single thread, program order (statement A before B → A HB B). (2) A mutex unlock HB the next lock of the same mutex. (3) A release store HB an acquire load that reads the stored value. (4) thread.join() HB the statements after it. (5) thread start HB the first statement of the started thread. Data races are defined as two conflicting accesses with no HB relation between them. The memory model of a language (C++11, Java, Go) is defined entirely in terms of HB — it defines what values a read can legally return. Without HB, a compiler is free to reorder, cache, or eliminate writes — even ones you believe are 'obviously' visible.
senior_nuance: HB is not transitive across relaxed atomics — a chain of relaxed loads and stores does not create HB. This surprises engineers who assume 'the value was written, so it must be visible.' The OOTA (out-of-thin-air) problem is an active area of memory model research — some models technically allow values to appear before they are written, which all real implementations avoid but formal models must rule out explicitly.
quote_to_say: No happens-before means no visibility guarantee — period.

front: What is false sharing, how does it degrade parallel performance, and how do you detect and fix it?
back: False sharing occurs when two threads write to different variables that reside on the same cache line (typically 64 bytes). The CPU cache coherence protocol (MESI) treats the cache line as the unit of ownership — when one thread writes, it must invalidate the cache line in all other CPUs holding it, forcing them to reload from L3/RAM even though they touch a different variable. Result: high cache-to-cache transfer overhead and linear slowdown instead of linear speedup. Detection: perf c2c (cache-to-cache) on Linux, or Intel VTune's 'False Sharing' analysis. Fix: pad structures so each thread's hot variable occupies its own cache line, or use alignas(64) / __cacheline_aligned. Java: @Contended annotation (requires -XX:-RestrictContended). Example: an array of counters incremented by N threads — put each counter in a padded struct.
senior_nuance: False sharing is especially dangerous in lock-free code where locks would serialize access and hide the problem. LMAX Disruptor is famous for careful cache-line alignment of ring buffer slots and sequence numbers. Thread-local storage (TLS) is the zero-cost solution when accumulation is possible: each thread writes to its own counter, then results are merged at the end.
quote_to_say: False sharing is when threads fight over a cache line that neither actually shares — hardware overhead, invisible in code.

front: Compare MVCC and two-phase locking (2PL) for database concurrency control. What does each sacrifice?
back: Two-Phase Locking (2PL): transactions acquire all locks before releasing any (growing phase then shrinking phase). Strict 2PL holds all write locks until commit. Guarantees serializability. Readers block writers; writers block readers. Deadlocks are possible and must be detected/resolved. Throughput degrades under high write contention. MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control): instead of locking, each write creates a new version of the data; readers see a consistent snapshot from their transaction start time and never block on writers. Writers create new versions rather than overwriting. Readers never block writers; writers never block readers for reads. Sacrifices: MVCC requires garbage collection of old versions (vacuum in PostgreSQL, purge in InnoDB) — if long-running transactions pin old snapshots, version chains grow unboundedly (the 'long transaction bloat' problem). MVCC alone does not prevent write-write conflicts (lost update) — databases add predicate locking or SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) on top.
senior_nuance: PostgreSQL's SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) tracks anti-dependency edges between transactions to detect serialization anomalies without locking — this achieves full serializability with MVCC's read performance. The tradeoff: SSI aborts transactions more aggressively than 2PL under some workloads. CockroachDB and YugabyteDB implement distributed MVCC with HLC (hybrid logical clocks) for causality tracking across nodes.
quote_to_say: MVCC makes readers and writers independent — the cost is version GC and snapshot management.

front: Define lock-free and wait-free. What guarantees does each provide, and what are the implementation costs?
back: Lock-free: the system as a whole makes progress — at least one thread completes an operation in a finite number of steps, even if individual threads are delayed indefinitely. Typical implementation: CAS (compare-and-swap) loop — if CAS fails (another thread won the race), retry. A single starved thread does not block others, but it may spin indefinitely under high contention. Wait-free: every thread completes its operation in a bounded number of steps, regardless of other threads' speeds. Stronger than lock-free — no thread can be starved. Implementation: typically requires helping protocols (a thread that sees an in-progress operation helps complete it before proceeding) or per-thread slots. Cost: significantly more complex; often slower than lock-free in practice due to helping overhead. Lock-free > blocking (mutexes) for throughput under contention; wait-free > lock-free for latency guarantees (real-time systems).
senior_nuance: Most 'lock-free' data structures in production (MPSC queues, hazard-pointer lists) are lock-free but not wait-free. Folly's MPMCQueue and the LMAX Disruptor use lock-free ring buffers. Memory reclamation is the hardest part of lock-free code: you cannot free a node if another thread might be reading it. Solutions: hazard pointers (O(n) overhead per thread), epoch-based reclamation (used in crossbeam-epoch in Rust), or RCU (read-copy-update, Linux kernel staple).
quote_to_say: Lock-free means the system progresses; wait-free means every thread progresses.

front: What is a condition variable and why must it always be used with a mutex? What is a spurious wakeup?
back: A condition variable allows threads to wait for a predicate to become true without busy-waiting. The pattern: lock mutex, check predicate, if false call cond_wait (which atomically releases the mutex and sleeps). When another thread signals (cond_signal/cond_broadcast), the waiter re-acquires the mutex and re-checks the predicate. The mutex is mandatory because: the check-then-wait must be atomic with respect to the signaler. Without the mutex, a signaler could signal between the check and the wait, causing the waiter to sleep forever (lost wakeup). Spurious wakeup: the OS may wake a waiting thread for no reason (allowed by POSIX to simplify kernel implementation). This is why the predicate must be checked in a loop (while(!predicate) { cond_wait(...); }), not an if statement. Languages: Java's Object.wait/notify, Go's sync.Cond, C++ std::condition_variable.
senior_nuance: cond_signal wakes one waiting thread; cond_broadcast wakes all. Using signal when broadcast is needed causes missed wakeups when multiple threads can make progress. Using broadcast when only one can make progress causes the thundering herd problem — N threads wake, N-1 go back to sleep after finding the predicate false. Production code often uses semaphores or channels (Go) instead, which compose more safely.
quote_to_say: Always check the predicate in a while loop — spurious wakeups are allowed by spec.

front: State Amdahl's Law and explain why it limits parallel speedup even with infinite threads.
back: Amdahl's Law: if a fraction P of a program can be parallelized, the maximum speedup with N threads is 1 / ((1-P) + P/N). As N → ∞, speedup approaches 1/(1-P). If 5% of the program is serial, the maximum possible speedup is 20× — regardless of core count. Implications: small serial fractions become dominant bottlenecks at scale. Lock contention, sequential I/O, and serialized coordination are all serial fractions. Gustafson's Law (the optimistic counterpart): as problem size grows with thread count, the serial fraction shrinks — parallelism is more effective for scaling work, not fixed work. Real systems hit Amdahl limits through: global lock contention, sequential log writes, single-threaded event loops, and centralized schedulers.
senior_nuance: Amdahl's Law applies at the hardware level too: memory bandwidth is the serial bottleneck for memory-bound workloads. Adding cores does not help if all threads compete for the same DRAM channels. NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) architectures expose this — a thread accessing memory on a remote NUMA node pays 2-3× the latency. NUMA-aware memory allocation (numactl, libnuma) pins threads and their memory to the same node.
quote_to_say: The serial 5% you ignored will cap your speedup at 20× forever.

front: What is a futex, and how does it make uncontended mutex acquisition essentially free?
back: A futex (fast userspace mutex) is a kernel primitive that allows userspace synchronization without a syscall in the common (uncontended) case. The kernel provides two operations: FUTEX_WAIT (sleep if the value at a memory address equals an expected value) and FUTEX_WAKE (wake threads waiting on an address). Uncontended lock: a single CAS on a userspace integer acquires the lock with no syscall. Contended lock: the loser CAS-es to mark the lock as 'has waiters' then calls FUTEX_WAIT, which is a syscall. On unlock, if the integer shows waiters, FUTEX_WAKE is called to unblock one. Result: uncontended mutex = ~1-4 ns (pure userspace CAS); contended mutex = syscall overhead (~200-500 ns). This is why pthreads mutexes and std::mutex on Linux are fast — they are futex-based. Spinlocks avoid the syscall but waste CPU in the contended case.
senior_nuance: Linux's robust futex list is a kernel-maintained list of futexes held by a thread. If a thread dies while holding a futex (crash, SIGKILL), the kernel detects this on the next waiter's FUTEX_WAIT and returns EOWNERDEAD — allowing the waiter to clean up. This prevents permanent deadlock from thread death without kernel-side lock awareness.
quote_to_say: A futex is a userspace CAS in the fast path — the kernel only wakes up when there is actual contention.

front: Compare the Actor model and CSP (Communicating Sequential Processes). How do Go channels fit?
back: Actor model (Erlang, Akka): each actor has a private mailbox and communicates by sending messages to named actors. Actors process messages sequentially; no shared state. Actors can create other actors and change behavior. Location transparency — an actor reference works across processes/machines. Failure isolation: one actor crash does not propagate (supervisor trees restart failed actors). CSP (Hoare, 1978): processes communicate through channels. No actor identity — communication is channel-centric, not actor-centric. Rendezvous by default: sender and receiver synchronize (unbuffered channel). Go implements CSP: goroutines are lightweight processes; channels are typed; select multiplexes over channels. Go's mantra: 'Do not communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating.' Goroutines are cheaper than OS threads (~2KB stack vs 1–8MB) because the Go runtime multiplexes them onto OS threads (M:N scheduling).
senior_nuance: Pure CSP and actors both avoid shared state — the key insight is that the concurrency unit controls its own data. Go departs from pure CSP by also allowing shared memory (sync.Mutex) — the language permits both models. Erlang's actor model with supervision trees (OTP) is the gold standard for fault-tolerant distributed systems: thousands of actors, each isolated, each restarted independently on failure.
quote_to_say: Actors name each other; CSP channels name no one — Go chose channels.

front: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming, and how is it prevented?
back: ABA occurs with CAS-based lock-free data structures. Scenario: Thread 1 reads value A from location X, prepares a CAS to change X from A to C. Thread 2 runs, changes X from A to B, then back to A. Thread 1's CAS succeeds (X is still A), but the underlying structure has changed — a node may have been freed and reallocated, making A point to a different object. The CAS cannot detect this reuse. Prevention strategies: (1) Tagged pointers — pair the pointer with a version counter in a double-word CAS (DWCAS/CMPXCHG16B on x86). The tag increments on every modification; ABA with matching tag is astronomically unlikely. (2) Hazard pointers — before accessing a pointer, publish it in a per-thread hazard array; memory cannot be freed if any hazard pointer references it. (3) Epoch-based reclamation — defer freeing until no thread is in the epoch that accessed the node. (4) Use a GC language — garbage collection eliminates ABA for heap pointers (though not for index-based structures).
senior_nuance: Java's AtomicStampedReference implements tagged-pointer CAS in userspace (the stamp is the version counter). Rust's crossbeam crate uses epoch-based reclamation. The ABA problem is the primary reason lock-free data structures are hard — not the CAS logic itself, but the memory reclamation around it. Most production lock-free code uses a memory reclamation scheme as a foundational layer.
quote_to_say: CAS sees A and thinks nothing changed — but the world changed and came back.

front: What is priority inversion and how did it crash the Mars Pathfinder?
back: Priority inversion: a high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task, while a medium-priority task preempts the low-priority task and runs instead — the high-priority task effectively runs at the medium task's priority. Mars Pathfinder (1997): a low-priority meteorological task held a mutex protecting a shared information bus. A high-priority task needed the bus. A medium-priority task preempted the low-priority holder. The VxWorks watchdog detected the high-priority task waiting too long and reset the system. Root cause: mutex lacked priority inheritance. Fix applied remotely: enable priority inheritance on the mutex (the kernel temporarily boosts the lock holder's priority to match the highest waiter). Prevention: (1) priority inheritance (PI) mutexes — kernel protocol PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT, (2) priority ceiling — mutex has a ceiling priority, holder runs at ceiling while holding it, (3) avoid shared resources between tasks of very different priorities.
senior_nuance: Priority inheritance is not free — the kernel must traverse the dependency chain when boosting (a task holding mutex A while waiting on mutex B may need its priority boosted transitively). Deeply nested lock chains can make inheritance expensive. Real-time systems (PREEMPT_RT Linux) implement full priority inheritance chains. Embedded RTOSes often use priority ceiling instead for bounded overhead.
quote_to_say: Priority inversion inverts the priority hierarchy — medium beats high because of low.
